metadata.analyticsNote: 7 hard questions identified from CAET Exam Analytics (sub-70% pass rate). Questions rewritten from different angles to teach reasoning, not memorize stems.

questions.learningObjective: Identify 14 CFR Part 43 as the part that contains performance rules for aircraft maintenance
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that 14 CFR Part 43 — Maintenance, Preventive Maintenance, Rebuilding, and Alteration — contains the performance rules that any person performing aircraft maintenance must follow.

flashcard.definition: 14 CFR Part 43 — titled 'Maintenance, Preventive Maintenance, Rebuilding, and Alteration' — contains the federal performance rules that any person performing aircraft maintenance must follow. It defines who may perform maintenance, how it must be performed, and what records must be kept. Repair stations operate under Part 145 but still perform maintenance per the rules in Part 43.
flashcard.analogy: Part 43 is the 'how to perform maintenance' rulebook. Part 145 is the 'how to operate a repair station' rulebook. A repair station must follow BOTH.

jeopardy.question: This is the 14 CFR part that outlines the performance rules for aircraft maintenance.
choices: Part 43
Part 21
Part 91
Part 147
jeopardy.explanation: Part 43 contains the performance rules for maintenance, preventive maintenance, rebuilding, and alteration. Part 21 covers certification procedures for products and articles. Part 91 covers general operating rules. Part 147 covers aviation maintenance technician schools.

multipleChoice.question: Which 14 CFR part outlines the performance rules that a repair station must follow when performing aircraft maintenance?
choices: Part 43, which contains the performance rules for maintenance, preventive maintenance
Part 21, which covers certification procedures for products and articles
Part 91, which covers general operating and flight rules
Part 147, which covers aviation maintenance technician schools
multipleChoice.explanation: Part 43 is the authoritative source for how maintenance must be performed. Repair stations also operate under Part 145, but the performance rules themselves — methods, techniques, record-keeping — come from Part 43. Parts 21, 91, and 147 address different subjects.

practice.question: A new technician at a repair station asks which federal regulation defines how maintenance tasks must actually be performed. What is the correct answer?
choices: Part 43 — performance rules for maintenance, preventive maintenance, and alteration
Part 21, which covers certification of aeronautical products and parts
Part 91, which addresses flight rules rather than maintenance performance
Part 147, which governs aviation maintenance technician school curricula
practice.explanation: Part 43 is the core maintenance-performance regulation. It applies to every person performing maintenance on certificated aircraft — including mechanics, repair stations, and operators. Part 145 specifies how repair stations are organized, but the how-to of maintenance itself lives in Part 43.

preassessment.question: Which 14 CFR part outlines the performance rules for aircraft maintenance?
choices: Part 43
Part 21
Part 91
Part 147

questions.learningObjective: Describe what 14 CFR Part 43 states about preventive maintenance
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that 14 CFR Part 43 defines preventive maintenance as the simple or minor preservation operations listed in Part 43 Appendix A, and that under certain conditions a certificated pilot may perform preventive maintenance on aircraft they own or operate.

flashcard.definition: 14 CFR Part 43 defines preventive maintenance as simple or minor preservation operations and the replacement of small standard parts not involving complex assembly. The specific items that qualify are listed in Part 43 Appendix A (Subpart (c)). A certificated pilot who holds at least a private pilot certificate may perform preventive maintenance on an aircraft they own or operate, except when operated under Part 121, 129, or 135.
flashcard.analogy: Preventive maintenance is the narrow list of 'owner-allowed' maintenance actions — simple items like oil changes, tire servicing, and lubrication — not structural or complex repairs.

jeopardy.question: According to 14 CFR Part 43, this statement about preventive maintenance is correct.
choices: Preventive maintenance includes any repair as long as the technician deems it minor
Only A&P mechanics may perform preventive maintenance
Preventive maintenance consists of simple or minor preservation operations listed in Part 43 Appendix A
Preventive maintenance requires a Designated Engineering Representative's signature
jeopardy.explanation: Preventive maintenance is specifically defined by the list in Part 43 Appendix A. A certificated pilot (at least Private) may perform preventive maintenance on their own aircraft under the conditions stated in Part 43. It is not 'anything minor' by technician judgment, and it does not require DER involvement.

multipleChoice.question: According to 14 CFR Part 43, which statement correctly describes preventive maintenance?
choices: Preventive maintenance includes any repair the technician considers minor
Only certificated A&P mechanics may perform preventive maintenance
Preventive maintenance consists of the simple or minor preservation operations listed in Part 43 Appendix A
Preventive maintenance must be approved in advance by a Designated Engineering Representative
multipleChoice.explanation: Preventive maintenance is explicitly defined by the list in Part 43 Appendix A — simple items like oil servicing, tire changes, battery servicing, and similar operations. A pilot who holds at least a Private certificate may perform these on their own aircraft, with documentation per Part 43. Not every 'minor' task qualifies, and DER approval is not required.

practice.question: A private pilot who owns a light aircraft asks whether they can legally change the engine oil themselves. What is the correct answer under 14 CFR Part 43?
choices: No — only A&P mechanics may service engine oil on any aircraft
Yes — any pilot can perform maintenance they feel comfortable doing
Yes — it is preventive maintenance allowed for pilot-owners
No — engine oil changes require DER sign-off and Form 337
practice.explanation: Engine oil changes are a classic example of preventive maintenance explicitly permitted to pilot-owners by Part 43 Appendix A. The pilot must hold at least a Private certificate, must own or operate the aircraft (not operated under Part 121, 129, or 135), and must document the work in the logbook. Not every maintenance activity qualifies — the list in Appendix A is specific.

preassessment.question: What does Part 43 say about preventive maintenance?
choices: Any repair a technician deems to be minor
Only A&P mechanics may perform this work
Simple or minor operations in Appendix A
Requires DER approval before being performed
preassessment.explanation: Part 43 defines preventive maintenance as the simple or minor operations listed in Appendix A.

questions.learningObjective: Describe what constitutes a major alteration per 14 CFR Part 43 Appendix A
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that Part 43 Appendix A defines major alterations as changes to type-design items (airframe, powerplant, propeller, appliance) that might appreciably affect weight, balance, structural strength, performance, flight characteristics, or other qualities affecting airworthiness, and that are not listed in the manufacturer's maintenance manual.

flashcard.definition: 14 CFR Part 43 Appendix A defines a major alteration as a change to type-design items — airframe, powerplant, propeller, or appliance — that might appreciably affect weight, balance, structural strength, performance, powerplant operation, flight characteristics, or other qualities affecting airworthiness, and that is not done by approved data in the manufacturer's maintenance manual. Major alterations require approved data and typically documentation on FAA Form 337.
flashcard.analogy: A major alteration is a change significant enough that it could affect how the aircraft flies or how safe it is — not just a like-for-like replacement.

jeopardy.question: According to 14 CFR Part 43 Appendix A, this describes a major alteration.
choices: Any task that takes more than one technician to complete
Replacement of any part, regardless of impact
A change to type-design items that might appreciably affect weight
An alteration that costs more than a specified dollar threshold
jeopardy.explanation: Appendix A classifies alterations as major based on the potential effect on airworthiness-related qualities, not labor, cost, or any-part replacement. Major alterations require approved data and documentation on Form 337.

multipleChoice.question: Part 43 Appendix A classifies certain changes to type-design items as a 'major alteration.' Which description fits that classification?
choices: Any maintenance task requiring more than one technician
Replacement of any part, regardless of its impact
A change to type-design items that might appreciably affect weight
An alteration that exceeds a specified dollar cost threshold
multipleChoice.explanation: Major alterations are defined by their potential effect on airworthiness — weight, balance, structural strength, performance, and flight characteristics — and by whether they are covered by approved data. They require approved data (AMM, STC, DER) and FAA Form 337. Labor or cost thresholds do not determine this classification.

practice.question: A technician plans to install a new engine that is not on the aircraft's type certificate data sheet (TCDS). Is this a major alteration, and what documentation is required?
choices: No — an engine swap is routine and needs only a logbook entry
No — the engine is simply a replacement part under warranty
Yes — a major alteration needing approved data and Form 337
Yes — but only a logbook entry is needed to document the swap
practice.explanation: An engine not on the TCDS is a textbook major alteration — it changes a type-design component (powerplant) in a way that may appreciably affect performance, weight/balance, and flight characteristics. Approved data (STC, DER field approval, or manufacturer data) must be used, and the work must be documented on FAA Form 337. A logbook entry alone is insufficient.

preassessment.question: What is a major alteration per Part 43 Appendix A?
choices: Any task requiring more than one tech
Replacement of any part
A change affecting airworthiness qualities not
Any alteration above a cost threshold

questions.learningObjective: Describe the required content of a maintenance record entry per 14 CFR §43.9
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that 14 CFR §43.9 requires a maintenance record entry to include: description of the work performed, date of completion, name of the person performing the work, and the signature, certificate number, and kind of certificate of the person approving the aircraft for return to service.

flashcard.definition: A maintenance record entry under 14 CFR §43.9 must include: (1) a description of the work performed or reference to data acceptable to the Administrator, (2) the date of completion, (3) the name of the person performing the work (if different from the approver), and (4) the signature, certificate number, and kind of certificate of the person approving the aircraft for return to service.
flashcard.analogy: Part 43.9 is the 'what must be written in the logbook' checklist — no element is optional.

jeopardy.question: Per 14 CFR §43.9, these four elements must be in every maintenance record entry.
choices: Only the date and the technician's initials on the page
Work description, completion date, name, approving signature
The total number of labor hours spent on the work order
The customer's name, address, and registered phone number
jeopardy.explanation: Section 43.9 specifies the four required elements: description of work, date of completion, name of person, and approving signature. Time spent and customer contact are not required by §43.9.

multipleChoice.question: What must be included in a maintenance record entry per 14 CFR §43.9?
choices: Only the date of work and the technician's initials
Description, date, person performing work, and signature
The total number of labor hours charged for the work done
The customer's name, mailing address, and contact phone number
multipleChoice.explanation: Part 43.9 requires four specific elements in every maintenance record entry: work description, completion date, person performing the work, and approving signature/certificate information. Labor hours and customer contact data are not part of the regulatory requirement.

practice.question: A technician repairs a VOR receiver and writes in the logbook: 'VOR repaired. 3 hours.' Is this entry compliant with 14 CFR §43.9?
choices: Yes, because any entry mentioning the work performed is sufficient
No — missing description of work, date, name, and approving signature
Yes, because listing the labor hours satisfies the §43.9 requirement
No, but only because the labor hours are not broken out by task
practice.explanation: The entry fails §43.9 on multiple counts: no description of what was done to the VOR, no date of completion, no name of the person performing the work, and no approving signature with certificate number and kind of certificate. A compliant entry would read: 'Replaced failed U14 driver IC, functional check satisfactory per AMM section 5-3. Signed, [name], [cert type], [cert number], [date].'

preassessment.question: What must a maintenance record entry include per §43.9?
choices: Date and initials only
Description, date, person
Labor hours only
Customer name and address

questions.learningObjective: Identify who is authorized to perform pitot-static and transponder inspections per 14 CFR Part 91.411 and 91.413
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that 14 CFR 91.411 and 91.413 require pitot-static and transponder inspections to be performed by a certificated repair station with the appropriate ratings, a certificated airframe-rated mechanic in some cases, or the manufacturer of the aircraft under specific conditions.

flashcard.definition: 14 CFR 91.411 (altimeter system and altitude reporting equipment) and 91.413 (ATC transponder) specify that these inspections must be performed by: (1) a certificated repair station with the appropriate ratings, (2) the manufacturer of the aircraft or equipment (under specified conditions), or (3) for pitot-static only, a certificated airframe-rated mechanic can perform SOME checks but not the 24-month pitot-static certification itself, which generally requires a repair station.
flashcard.analogy: These inspections are a narrower authority than general maintenance — only specifically-rated people can sign them off.

jeopardy.question: This is who is authorized to perform pitot-static and transponder inspections under 14 CFR 91.411 and 91.413.
choices: Any certificated pilot who owns the aircraft
A certificated repair station with the appropriate ratings
Any A&P mechanic with general maintenance privileges
No one — only the FAA performs these inspections
jeopardy.explanation: 91.411 and 91.413 require appropriately-rated repair stations or the manufacturer. General pilot or A&P privileges do not extend to the 24-month certifications for altimeter/pitot-static and transponder systems. The FAA does not perform maintenance inspections.

multipleChoice.question: Who is authorized to perform a pitot-static and transponder inspection under 14 CFR 91.411 and 91.413?
choices: Any certificated pilot who owns the aircraft
A certificated repair station with the appropriate ratings
Any A&P mechanic operating under general maintenance privileges
Only FAA inspectors during scheduled ramp checks
multipleChoice.explanation: The regulations specify appropriately-rated repair stations or the manufacturer. General A&P privileges do not extend to these 24-month certifications — they require specific instrument/accessory ratings. Pilots and FAA inspectors do not perform these inspections.

practice.question: An aircraft owner wants to have the required 24-month pitot-static and transponder certifications performed. Who can legally sign off these inspections?
choices: The owner, provided they hold a current Private Pilot certificate
A repair station with appropriate ratings, or the OEM under specified conditions
Any A&P mechanic working at the local general-aviation airport
No one locally — only FAA field offices may perform the certifications
practice.explanation: Parts 91.411 and 91.413 require an appropriately rated repair station or the manufacturer. A general A&P without the instrument/accessory rating cannot sign off the 24-month certifications. Owners and FAA field offices are not authorized to perform these inspections.

preassessment.question: Who can perform pitot-static and transponder inspections per 91.411/91.413?
choices: Any pilot-owner
A certificated repair
Any A&P mechanic
Only FAA inspectors

questions.learningObjective: Describe documentation requirements for altimeter and transponder tests
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that altimeter and transponder tests must be documented in the maintenance records with the date of the test, the maximum altitude tested (for altimeter), and the signature, certificate number, and kind of certificate of the person approving the aircraft for return to service, as required by 14 CFR §43.9 and Part 43 Appendix E/F.

flashcard.definition: Altimeter and transponder test results must be documented in the maintenance records with: the date of the test, the maximum altitude to which the altimeter was tested (altimeter only), and the signature, certificate number, and kind of certificate of the person approving the aircraft for return to service. Appendix E of Part 43 details the altimeter test; Appendix F details the transponder test.
flashcard.analogy: The test record is the evidence that the regulatory 24-month inspection was completed — without it, the aircraft cannot operate in Mode C airspace.

jeopardy.question: This is how an altimeter or transponder test must be documented in the maintenance records.
choices: With just a sticker on the instrument face
Verbally confirmed with the pilot
Only by the flight crew in the aircraft discrepancy log
With test date, maximum altitude tested, and signature
jeopardy.explanation: Proper documentation includes the test date, maximum altitude (altimeter only), and the approving signature/certificate data of the person returning the unit to service. Stickers alone are informational but do not satisfy the record-keeping requirement. Verbal confirmation and pilot-side logs do not substitute for the maintenance record entry.

multipleChoice.question: What entries must appear in the maintenance records after an altimeter or transponder performance test is completed?
choices: By placing a sticker on the instrument panel
Verbally to the pilot at aircraft pickup
Only in the aircraft's flight discrepancy log
By an entry including the date of the test
multipleChoice.explanation: The maintenance record entry must include the test date, the maximum altitude tested (for altimeter), and the approving person's signature/certificate information. This satisfies the general §43.9 requirements plus the specific test appendices in Part 43.

practice.question: A technician has just completed the 24-month altimeter and transponder tests on an aircraft. What must go into the maintenance records?
choices: Only a note in the logbook saying that the tests were completed successfully
A sticker placed in the cockpit is sufficient documentation for these tests
No logbook entry is required — the test equipment certificate is sufficient
A §43.9 entry with test date, max altitude, technician signature, and number
practice.explanation: The maintenance record entry is mandatory. It must include the required elements per §43.9 and the specific test-related data per Appendices E (altimeter) and F (transponder): the date of each test, the maximum altitude to which the altimeter was tested, and the technician's signature, certificate number, and kind of certificate approving the return to service. Stickers, raw test certificates, or abbreviated notes do not satisfy the regulatory requirement.

preassessment.question: How must altimeter/transponder test results be documented?
choices: Sticker on panel
Verbal to pilot
Pilot's flight log only
Entry with date, max

questions.learningObjective: Classify an installation of a TSO part when the installation is covered by an STC and flight manual supplement
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that installing a TSO part per an STC that includes a flight manual supplement is generally considered a major alteration because the STC route typically involves airworthiness-affecting changes that require approved data and documentation on Form 337.

flashcard.definition: When a TSO (Technical Standard Order) part is installed under an STC (Supplemental Type Certificate) that includes a flight manual supplement (AFM supplement), the installation is generally considered a major alteration. The STC provides the approved data, and the AFM supplement indicates the installation affects aircraft operating procedures or performance. FAA Form 337 is typically required.
flashcard.analogy: An STC with an AFM supplement is saying 'this installation changes how the aircraft operates' — that is a classic signature of a major alteration.

jeopardy.question: If a TSO part has an STC for installation AND the STC includes a flight manual supplement, this describes the installation.
choices: A minor alteration that requires only a logbook entry
Preventive maintenance that a pilot may perform
An operation requiring a DER field approval with no Form 337
A major alteration requiring approved data (the STC) and typically FAA Form
jeopardy.explanation: The presence of an STC with a flight manual supplement strongly indicates a major alteration — the installation affects operating procedures or performance enough to warrant an AFM supplement. Approved data (the STC) is used, and Form 337 is typically required.

multipleChoice.question: A TSO-approved unit is installed per an STC, and the STC issues a flight manual supplement. How is this installation classified?
choices: A minor alteration — only a logbook entry is required
Preventive maintenance — a pilot may perform it
A DER field approval with no Form 337 required
A major alteration
multipleChoice.explanation: An STC with an AFM supplement is a classic indicator of a major alteration. The AFM supplement exists because the installation changes how the aircraft must be operated — a condition that requires the major-alteration treatment, including Form 337. A logbook entry alone is insufficient.

practice.question: A technician installs a new TSO-approved autopilot per an STC kit that includes a flight manual supplement. What classification and paperwork apply?
choices: Minor alteration — logbook entry only is sufficient documentation
Preventive maintenance — the aircraft owner may sign it off
No documentation is needed because the autopilot is a TSO part
Major alteration — STC, Form 337, AFM supplement, logbook entry
practice.explanation: STC-installed autopilot with an AFM supplement is major. Use the STC as approved data, follow the installation manual, and complete FAA Form 337 in addition to the logbook entry. The flight manual supplement must also be placed with the aircraft documents. Logbook entry alone is not sufficient, and neither pilots nor TSO status alone authorize an alteration of this scope.

preassessment.question: TSO part installed with STC and AFM supplement — major or minor?
choices: Minor (logbook only)
Preventive maintenance
DER field approval, no Form 337
Major (approved data + Form 337)

questions.learningObjective: Classify a TSO-to-TSO part replacement
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that direct replacement of a TSO (Technical Standard Order) part with another TSO part of the same form, fit, and function is generally NOT a major alteration and may be accomplished under standard maintenance practices with a logbook entry — provided the replacement is installed per the manufacturer's maintenance manual.

flashcard.definition: Replacing one TSO part with another TSO part of the same form, fit, and function — using the manufacturer's maintenance manual procedures — is generally NOT a major alteration. It is a standard maintenance action documented in the logbook per §43.9. A major alteration classification applies only if the replacement introduces a change that might appreciably affect weight, balance, performance, or flight characteristics.
flashcard.analogy: Swapping one TSO radio for another equivalent TSO radio is like replacing one light bulb with an identical bulb — it is maintenance, not alteration.

jeopardy.question: If a TSO part is replaced with another TSO part of equivalent form, fit, and function, this is how the installation is generally classified.
choices: Generally NOT a major alteration
A major alteration requiring Form 337
An alteration requiring an STC
Preventive maintenance that a pilot-owner may perform
jeopardy.explanation: Like-for-like TSO replacement is maintenance, not a major alteration. A logbook entry suffices. An STC is not needed for an equivalent TSO part. Pilot-owner preventive maintenance is a separate narrow category not including general avionics replacement.

multipleChoice.question: If a TSO part is replaced with another TSO part of equivalent form, fit, and function, is this generally considered a major alteration?
choices: Generally no — it is typically a maintenance action with a logbook entry per §43.9
Yes, any TSO part replacement is a major alteration requiring Form 337
Yes, an STC is required for every TSO part replacement
No, because pilot-owners may perform any TSO replacement as preventive maintenance
multipleChoice.explanation: Like-for-like TSO replacement is not a major alteration — no approved data beyond the maintenance manual is required, and Form 337 is not triggered. A proper §43.9 logbook entry is sufficient. Pilot-owner preventive maintenance is limited to the Appendix A list and does not generally include TSO avionics.

practice.question: A technician replaces a failed TSO-approved communication radio with another TSO-approved radio that has the same form, fit, and function, using the manufacturer's installation manual. What classification and documentation apply?
choices: Maintenance — document per §43.9 with a complete logbook entry
Major alteration — requires Form 337 and STC approved data
Preventive maintenance — the aircraft owner may sign it off
Nothing required if the new radio powers up and functions properly
practice.explanation: Like-for-like TSO replacement done per the manufacturer's manual is maintenance, not a major alteration. The required documentation is a §43.9-compliant logbook entry with a description of the work, date, name of the person performing the work, and the approving signature with certificate data. Pilot-owner preventive maintenance is not the applicable category, and documentation is always required for maintenance work.

preassessment.question: Is replacing a TSO part with another TSO part generally a major alteration?
choices: Generally no
Yes — always major
Requires an STC
Is preventive maintenance

questions.learningObjective: Identify what the acronym STC stands for
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that STC stands for Supplemental Type Certificate — an FAA-issued approval that authorizes a major design change to a type-certificated aircraft, engine, propeller, or appliance.

flashcard.definition: STC stands for Supplemental Type Certificate. It is an FAA-issued approval that authorizes a major design change to a type-certificated product (aircraft, engine, propeller, or appliance). The STC provides the approved data needed to make and document the change. STCs are owned by their holders and must be referenced when installations are made per their approval.
flashcard.analogy: An STC is like a formally approved 'mod kit' — the FAA has reviewed the design change and certified that it produces an airworthy result when installed as specified.

jeopardy.question: This is what the acronym STC stands for.
choices: Standard Technical Certificate
Specialized Tool Certification
Standard Testing Code
Supplemental Type Certificate
jeopardy.explanation: STC is Supplemental Type Certificate — the FAA's approval for a major design change to a type-certificated product.

multipleChoice.question: What does the acronym STC stand for?
choices: Standard Technical Certificate
Specialized Tool Certification
Standard Testing Code
Supplemental Type Certificate
multipleChoice.explanation: STC means Supplemental Type Certificate. It is an FAA approval for a major design change to a type-certificated aircraft, engine, propeller, or appliance. STC-installed modifications use the STC as approved data and typically require Form 337 documentation.

practice.question: A technician plans to install a new avionics package that the vendor describes as 'STC-approved for Cessna 172 variants.' What does the STC designation tell the technician?
choices: That any standard tools will work for the installation
That the avionics are cheaper than the OEM alternatives
That the avionics are certified only for experimental aircraft
That the FAA has issued approved data for installation on the listed aircraft
practice.explanation: STC-approved means the FAA has issued a Supplemental Type Certificate for the installation. The STC provides the approved data — installation manual, drawings, limitations — that must be followed exactly. The technician must use the STC's installation manual and required documentation. Pricing and experimental classification are unrelated to STC approval.

preassessment.question: What does STC stand for?
choices: Standard Technical Certificate
Specialized Tool Certification
Standard Testing Code
Supplemental Type Certificate

questions.learningObjective: Identify the typical recurrent training interval for Part 145 repair station personnel
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that most Part 145 training programs require recurrent training for repair station personnel at intervals not exceeding 24 months, with the specific interval and content defined in the station's FAA-accepted training program manual.

flashcard.definition: Most Part 145 training programs specify recurrent training for repair-station personnel at intervals not exceeding 24 months, with the specifics (subjects, evaluation criteria, documentation) defined in the station's FAA-accepted training program. Each repair station's program manual is the authoritative source for its own personnel.
flashcard.analogy: Two-year recurrent training is the common default — the specifics always come from the station's own approved training program.

jeopardy.question: According to most Part 145 training programs, this is the typical interval for recurrent training of repair station personnel.
choices: Not exceeding every 6 months
Not exceeding every 60 months
Only upon hire — no recurrent training required
Not exceeding every 24 months
jeopardy.explanation: A 24-month cycle is typical for Part 145 recurrent training. Shorter (6-month) intervals are uncommon. 60 months is too long to keep personnel current. Recurrent training is required — initial training alone does not satisfy Part 145 requirements.

multipleChoice.question: How often must repair-station personnel typically undergo recurrent training per most Part 145 training programs?
choices: At least every 6 months
At least every 60 months
Only upon initial hire — no recurrent training is required
At intervals not exceeding 24 months
multipleChoice.explanation: A 24-month recurrent training cycle is typical for Part 145 stations. The specific interval and subject content are defined in each station's FAA-accepted training program manual. Personnel can check their own training records against the station's program to confirm compliance.

practice.question: A technician at a Part 145 repair station notes that their last formal training was 26 months ago. What does most Part 145 guidance say about this?
choices: No concern — training is a one-time initial event only
The technician must be immediately terminated for the lapse
Training is only required for pilots, not avionics technicians
Likely overdue — Part 145 programs typically require recurrent every 24 months
practice.explanation: Most Part 145 programs specify recurrent training at intervals not exceeding 24 months. The station's training program manual is the authoritative source for the exact requirement — the technician should consult the manager and complete the required training. Termination is not the remedy for a training lapse; completing the training is. Training is explicitly required for repair-station technical personnel, not only pilots.

preassessment.question: How often must Part 145 personnel typically undergo recurrent training?
choices: Every 6 months
Every 60 months
Only upon hire
Every 24 months

questions.learningObjective: Describe the primary purpose of a Part 145 repair station's capability list
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a Part 145 repair station's capability list identifies the specific articles (by make, model, or other description) the station is approved to perform maintenance, preventive maintenance, or alterations on, under its ratings and within its approved procedures.

flashcard.definition: A capability list under Part 145 identifies the specific articles (by make, model, or other description) the repair station is authorized to perform maintenance, preventive maintenance, or alterations on under its certificate ratings. Items on the capability list must be supported by required documentation, personnel, tooling, and technical data.
flashcard.analogy: The capability list is the repair station's menu of work it is approved to do — adding a new item requires the right staff, tools, and data.

jeopardy.question: This is the primary purpose of a Part 145 repair station's capability list.
choices: To identify the station's employee roster
To identify the specific articles (by make, model, or description) the station
To list the station's pricing for each job
To record the aircraft the station has worked on historically
jeopardy.explanation: The capability list identifies what the station is certified to work on — by make/model or description. Employee rosters, pricing, and work history are separate records that do not constitute the capability list.

multipleChoice.question: What is the main purpose of a Part 145 repair station's capability list?
choices: To identify the station's current employees
To identify the specific articles the station is authorized to perform
To list the station's pricing for repair jobs
To record the aircraft the station has worked on in the past
multipleChoice.explanation: The capability list is a regulatory document that identifies which articles the station is certified to work on. Adding an item requires demonstrating the station has the personnel, tooling, data, and facilities to support it. Employee rosters, pricing, and historical logs are separate records.

practice.question: A repair station's capability list shows a specific radio model as 'within capabilities,' but the latest manual revision for that radio is not currently available in the shop. Can the station legally work on that radio?
choices: Yes — being on the capability list is the only requirement for the work
No — capability requires current technical data, which is not available
Yes — if the technician has prior experience with the radio model
Yes — if a note about the outdated data is placed in the work order
practice.explanation: Being on the capability list is necessary but not sufficient. Capability also includes the requirement that current technical data be available. Without the latest manual revision, the station cannot perform the work until the current data is obtained. Current data must be obtained before proceeding per Part 145 obligations.

preassessment.question: What is the purpose of a Part 145 capability list?
choices: List employees
Identify specific articles
List pricing
Record historical work

questions.learningObjective: Identify who is responsible for ensuring current technical data is available and used in a Part 145 repair station
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that under Part 145, the repair station is responsible for ensuring current technical data is available and used during maintenance, preventive maintenance, and alteration work — specifically, the station management and designated personnel (per the accepted procedures manual) must maintain current data as a condition of the station's certificate.

flashcard.definition: Under 14 CFR Part 145, the repair station itself is responsible for ensuring current technical data is available and used during maintenance. The station's accepted procedures manual designates specific personnel (typically including the Accountable Manager and Chief Inspector or equivalent) to maintain current manuals, service bulletins, and advisory data. Using outdated data violates Part 145 requirements.
flashcard.analogy: Current technical data is a continuous requirement — the station cannot do work correctly without it, and its maintenance is a responsibility of the station itself, not of a single technician.

jeopardy.question: Under Part 145, this party is responsible for ensuring that current technical data is available and used during avionics maintenance.
choices: The repair station itself
The individual aircraft owner
The FAA
The part manufacturer alone
jeopardy.explanation: Part 145 places the responsibility on the repair station. The station's accepted procedures manual designates specific personnel for this responsibility. The FAA oversees; it does not maintain individual stations' technical data. Part manufacturers publish the data but do not ensure it is in use at a specific station.

multipleChoice.question: For a Part 145 repair station, which party bears responsibility for keeping current technical data on hand and in use during avionics work?
choices: The repair station itself, through the designated
The individual aircraft owner
The FAA inspector assigned to the station
The part manufacturer alone
multipleChoice.explanation: The repair station bears this responsibility under Part 145. Specific personnel (typically the Accountable Manager and Chief Inspector, or equivalent positions in the station's procedures manual) are designated to ensure current data is on hand. The FAA provides oversight, not data management. Manufacturers publish the data but cannot ensure its use at individual stations.

practice.question: A technician at a Part 145 station finds that the current service bulletin for a GPS model is missing from the shop's technical data library. What is the correct course of action?
choices: Stop work until the current service bulletin is obtained and reviewed
Proceed with the work and note the missing data in the logbook
Search online aviation forums for the missing information content
Improvise the procedure based on the technician's past experience
practice.explanation: Part 145 requires current data to be in use. The repair station is responsible for ensuring current data is available. Notify the designated personnel (Accountable Manager, Chief Inspector, or as specified in the procedures manual) so the missing data can be obtained before work continues. Proceeding without current data, improvising, or using unofficial sources all violate Part 145 and risk improper work.

preassessment.question: Who ensures current technical data is available in a Part 145 shop?
choices: The repair station itself
Aircraft owner
The FAA
The part manufacturer

questions.learningObjective: Describe the primary purpose of FAA Form 8130-3 (Authorized Release Certificate / Airworthiness Approval Tag)
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that FAA Form 8130-3 is an Authorized Release Certificate (also called the Airworthiness Approval Tag) used to approve new or overhauled aircraft parts for return to service, providing traceability and airworthiness attestation that the part meets approved design data.

flashcard.definition: FAA Form 8130-3 is the Authorized Release Certificate (commonly called the Airworthiness Approval Tag). It certifies that new or overhauled aircraft parts conform to approved design data and are in a condition for safe operation. It provides traceability and is used to transfer airworthiness attestation when parts move between owners or installers.
flashcard.analogy: An 8130-3 tag is like a certificate of authenticity for an aircraft part — it documents that the part has been approved for return to service.

jeopardy.question: This is the primary purpose of FAA Form 8130-3 (Authorized Release Certificate / Airworthiness Approval Tag).
choices: To approve new or overhauled aircraft parts for return to service
To record a certificated pilot's most recent biennial flight review
To document the current aircraft registration status with the FAA
To serve as a sales invoice for aircraft parts shipped from vendors
jeopardy.explanation: The 8130-3 is an airworthiness-approval and traceability document for aircraft parts. It is not related to pilot flight reviews, aircraft registration, or sales invoicing (though the tag often accompanies sales of new or overhauled parts).

multipleChoice.question: An avionics unit arrives at the shop with an FAA Form 8130-3 attached. What does that tag primarily certify about the unit?
choices: To certify that new or overhauled aircraft parts conform
To record a pilot's flight review completion
To document an aircraft's registration
To act as a sales invoice for aircraft parts
multipleChoice.explanation: Form 8130-3 certifies that aircraft parts meet approved design data and are airworthy. The tag provides traceability that travels with the part. It is not a flight-review, registration, or sales document — though it commonly accompanies sales of new or overhauled parts.

practice.question: A technician receives an avionics unit with an FAA Form 8130-3 tag attached. What does that tag tell the technician?
choices: Conformance to approved design data and approval for return to service
The unit was sold recently and remains within the manufacturer's warranty
The aircraft the unit is installed in is currently registered with the FAA
The pilot who flew the aircraft is current on their biennial flight review
practice.explanation: Form 8130-3 certifies conformance to approved design data and approval for return to service per the issuer's statement, giving the installer airworthiness traceability for the part. Warranty, aircraft registration, and pilot currency are separate matters with separate documentation.

preassessment.question: What is FAA Form 8130-3 used for?
choices: Certifying aircraft parts
Pilot flight reviews
Aircraft registration
Parts sales invoicing

questions.learningObjective: Identify the FAA's definition of maintenance
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that per 14 CFR Part 1, 'maintenance' means inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and the replacement of parts — but excludes preventive maintenance, which is separately defined.

flashcard.definition: 14 CFR Part 1 defines 'maintenance' as inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and the replacement of parts, BUT excludes preventive maintenance. Preventive maintenance is separately defined as the simple or minor preservation operations listed in Part 43 Appendix A.
flashcard.analogy: 'Maintenance' in the FAA sense is a broad category that INCLUDES many things but specifically EXCLUDES the narrow list of preventive-maintenance tasks.

jeopardy.question: According to FAA regulations, this is the definition of 'maintenance.'
choices: Any activity that keeps an aircraft operating safely
Inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, parts replacement
Only the replacement of parts that have failed in service
Any maintenance work performed by a certificated A&P mechanic
jeopardy.explanation: The Part 1 definition is specific: maintenance = inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement — but NOT preventive maintenance, which is defined separately. General phrases like 'any activity' or 'any work by an A&P' are not the regulatory definition.

multipleChoice.question: In 14 CFR Part 1, how is the term 'maintenance' defined for regulatory purposes?
choices: Any activity that keeps an aircraft operational in the fleet
Inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement
Only the replacement of failed parts in the airframe
Any work performed by a certificated A&P mechanic of any rating
multipleChoice.explanation: 14 CFR Part 1 defines maintenance as inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement, explicitly excluding preventive maintenance. Preventive maintenance is a separate category limited to the simple operations listed in Part 43 Appendix A.

practice.question: A student asks where the line is between 'maintenance' and 'preventive maintenance' in FAA regulations. What is the best answer?
choices: Both are the same — the two terms are fully interchangeable in FAA use
Maintenance is broad; preventive is the narrow Appendix A list only
Maintenance refers only to major repairs; everything else is preventive
The distinction is made by the individual technician's own judgment
practice.explanation: The FAA draws a clear regulatory distinction: maintenance (per Part 1) is a broad category — inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement. Preventive maintenance is a separate narrower category consisting only of the simple or minor preservation operations listed in Part 43 Appendix A. The distinction matters because preventive maintenance can be performed by certain pilot-owners, while general maintenance requires appropriately certificated mechanics or repair stations.

preassessment.question: What is the FAA definition of 'maintenance'?
choices: Any activity keeping an aircraft operating
Inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts
Only replacing failed parts
Any A&P-performed work

questions.learningObjective: Explain why Part 145 repair stations must keep work areas and walkways clear of clutter
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that Part 145 requires repair stations to maintain clean, organized work areas and clear walkways because clutter creates safety hazards (trip, fall, fire) and quality risks (FOD, tool loss, damaged parts).

flashcard.definition: Part 145 requires repair stations to maintain clean, organized work areas and clear walkways. Clutter creates both safety hazards (trip, fall, fire hazards from combustibles) and quality risks (FOD, lost tools, damaged parts, inability to find components). Housekeeping is a recurring Part 145 compliance item.
flashcard.analogy: A cluttered shop is dangerous and unreliable — both safety and workmanship suffer when basic organization lapses.

jeopardy.question: This is why a Part 145 repair station must keep walkways and work areas free of clutter.
choices: For visual appeal during FAA inspections only
Because Part 145 has no such requirement
To comply with OSHA but not Part 145
To reduce safety hazards
jeopardy.explanation: Housekeeping addresses both safety and quality. Part 145 does include housekeeping-related requirements, and OSHA adds workplace safety requirements — the two are complementary, not alternatives. Visual appeal during inspections is a side effect, not the primary reason.

multipleChoice.question: Part 145 shops enforce strict housekeeping of walkways and work areas. What does that housekeeping requirement primarily protect against?
choices: For visual appeal during FAA audits only
Clutter is not actually a concern in Part 145 shops
Only OSHA requires clean walkways — Part 145 has no housekeeping rules
Because clutter creates real safety hazards
multipleChoice.explanation: Housekeeping in a Part 145 shop matters for safety (trips, falls, fire loads) and quality (FOD control, tool accountability, parts integrity). Part 145 includes housekeeping-related requirements, and OSHA layers additional safety requirements on top. Both apply simultaneously.

practice.question: A technician notices that the shop's main walkway has accumulated old parts boxes, cable offcuts, and a few spilled fluid-absorbent rags. Is this a Part 145 issue, and what is the appropriate response?
choices: No — as long as the boxes are not on the work benches themselves
No — repair stations are not subject to housekeeping requirements
Yes — but only if the FAA inspector is scheduled to visit that day
Yes — clutter creates safety, FOD, and fire hazards requiring cleanup
practice.explanation: Cluttered walkways are a Part 145 issue plus a safety concern. They create trip and fall hazards, quality risks (FOD, part loss), and fire danger — fluid-soaked rags can self-heat. Immediate response: clear the walkway, properly dispose of the rags, and follow up with responsible personnel to prevent recurrence. The rule applies continuously — not only when FAA inspectors are present.

preassessment.question: Why must Part 145 shops keep walkways clear?
choices: For visual appeal and shop aesthetics only
No such requirement exists under the rules
Under OSHA standards only, not Part 145
To reduce safety and quality risks
preassessment.explanation: Part 145 shops must keep walkways clear to reduce safety hazards and protect work quality.

questions.learningObjective: Describe pre-employment drug testing requirements under a Part 145 station's FAA-approved program
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that if a Part 145 station's FAA-approved antidrug program includes pre-employment drug testing, a technician must complete and pass that test prior to beginning work in a safety-sensitive position, in compliance with 14 CFR Part 120.

flashcard.definition: Under 14 CFR Part 120 and when incorporated into a Part 145 station's FAA-approved antidrug program, a prospective technician in a safety-sensitive position must complete and pass a pre-employment drug test before beginning work. The station's specific program details (testing substances, testing laboratory, consequences) are documented per Part 120 requirements.
flashcard.analogy: Pre-employment drug testing is a standard safety-sensitive hiring gate — complete and pass before starting the job.

jeopardy.question: If a Part 145 station's FAA-approved antidrug program includes pre-employment drug testing, this is required of a new technician.
choices: Complete and pass the pre-employment drug test before starting work
Ignore the test if they feel confident they would pass
Complete the test at any time during the first year of employment
Self-certify on an honor system
jeopardy.explanation: Pre-employment testing must be completed and passed BEFORE starting work in a safety-sensitive position. It cannot be deferred, ignored, or self-certified. Part 120 sets the federal requirements; Part 145 stations incorporate them into their programs.

multipleChoice.question: If a pre-employment drug test is part of a Part 145 station's FAA-approved antidrug program, what must the technician do?
choices: Complete and pass the pre-employment drug test before starting work
Ignore the test if they are confident they would pass
Complete the test at any point within the first year of employment
Self-certify on an honor system that they are drug-free
multipleChoice.explanation: Pre-employment testing must be completed and passed before starting safety-sensitive duties. Deferral, avoidance, and self-certification are not acceptable under Part 120 or the station's program. This is a non-negotiable hiring gate for Part 145 technicians in safety-sensitive roles.

practice.question: A new technician has accepted a position at a Part 145 repair station whose FAA-approved antidrug program includes pre-employment testing. What is the correct sequence of events before the technician can begin work?
choices: Pass the pre-employment drug test before starting safety-sensitive duties
Start work immediately; the test can be completed at any convenient time
Begin work if the technician self-certifies they are drug-free on paper
Complete the drug test within one year of the start of employment date
practice.explanation: Pre-employment testing is a gating requirement. The sequence is: conditional offer, test, negative result, then start of safety-sensitive work. The technician must complete and pass the pre-employment drug test before beginning work in a safety-sensitive position. Starting work before the test is complete, or relying on self-certification, violates Part 120 and the station's approved program.

preassessment.question: If pre-employment drug testing is part of a Part 145 program, when must the technician complete it?
choices: Before beginning
Anytime within year 1
Only if they wish
Via self-certification

questions.learningObjective: Describe the calibration verification required before using specialized test equipment in a Part 145 shop
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that before using specialized test equipment in a Part 145 shop, a technician must confirm the equipment's calibration is current and traceable, per the station's approved procedures and the equipment manufacturer's calibration interval.

flashcard.definition: Before using specialized test equipment in a Part 145 shop, the technician must confirm the equipment's calibration is current and traceable to a recognized standard. The station's procedures manual and the equipment manufacturer's interval govern the required calibration cycle. Out-of-calibration equipment cannot produce valid test results and cannot be used for maintenance decisions.
flashcard.analogy: Using out-of-cal test equipment is like using a bent ruler — the measurement looks plausible but is not valid.

jeopardy.question: Before using any specialized test equipment in a Part 145 environment, this must be confirmed.
choices: The technician has used the equipment before
The equipment is the latest model
The equipment was purchased within the last year
The equipment's calibration is current and traceable
jeopardy.explanation: Calibration currency and traceability are the regulatory requirements. Familiarity, model age, or recent purchase do not substitute for verified calibration. Out-of-calibration equipment cannot be used for maintenance decisions.

multipleChoice.question: A technician pulls a piece of specialized test equipment from the shop's shared tool area in a Part 145 environment. What must be verified before the equipment can be used?
choices: That the technician has previously used this specific piece of equipment
That the equipment is the newest model available
That the equipment was purchased within the last year
That the equipment's calibration is current and traceable
multipleChoice.explanation: Calibration currency and traceability are required. The station's procedures manual specifies calibration intervals consistent with the manufacturer's recommendations. Out-of-calibration equipment cannot produce valid results and must not be used for maintenance decisions regardless of how familiar or new it is.

practice.question: A technician picks up a digital multimeter from the shop's shared tool area. The calibration sticker on the meter shows the last calibration was 14 months ago, and the sticker indicates the interval is 12 months. Can the technician use this meter?
choices: Yes — 14 months is close enough to 12 months for shop use
Yes — if the technician personally verifies it against a battery
Yes — if the technician documents the discrepancy in the work order
No — the meter is out of calibration and cannot be used for Part 145
practice.explanation: An instrument past its calibration date cannot produce valid measurements in a Part 145 environment, regardless of how small the overage. The meter must be sent for calibration before use, and the technician should select a different, in-calibration meter in the meantime. Battery-voltage spot-checks and documentation of the discrepancy do not substitute for formal calibration.

preassessment.question: Before using specialized test equipment in a Part 145 shop, confirm:
choices: Prior familiarity with the equipment
The equipment is the newest model
It was recently purchased
Calibration is current and traceable

questions.learningObjective: Identify the appropriate first troubleshooting step when a pilot reports intermittent VOR NAV flag indications
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the appropriate first troubleshooting step for intermittent VOR NAV flag indications in a Part 145 shop is to verify the basic installation — antenna, coax, connectors, and power — before replacing the receiver or other internal components.

flashcard.term: VOR NAV flag troubleshooting — first step
flashcard.definition: For intermittent VOR NAV flag indications, the first step in a Part 145 shop is to verify physical-layer items: antenna condition and secure mounting, coax integrity and connector tightness, and adequate power to the receiver. Most intermittent NAV flag issues trace to installation or wiring problems rather than receiver internal faults.
flashcard.analogy: Like any intermittent signal problem, start with the physical path — most causes are there, not in the electronics.

jeopardy.question: For intermittent VOR NAV flag indications, this is a useful first step a Part 145 technician can take.
choices: Immediately replace the VOR receiver
Only write up the squawk and return the aircraft to service
Rewrite the aircraft logbook
Verify physical-layer items first (antenna, coax, connectors, power)
jeopardy.explanation: Physical-layer checks are the first step for intermittent symptoms. Receiver replacement is expensive and often unnecessary. A write-up with no action is not a resolution. Logbook rewriting is not appropriate.

multipleChoice.question: A pilot reports intermittent NAV flag indications on a VOR receiver. Which step might a new technician take FIRST in a Part 145 shop?
choices: Immediately replace the VOR receiver
Write up the squawk and return the aircraft to service without action
Rewrite the aircraft's logbook
Verify the physical installation first — check antenna condition and mounting
multipleChoice.explanation: Intermittent symptoms typically originate in the physical layer — antennas, coax, connectors, and power feeds. These checks are cheap and often resolve the issue. Only after physical-layer items are verified should receiver internals be investigated. Replacing the receiver first is expensive and often unnecessary.

practice.question: A technician is troubleshooting intermittent NAV flag indications on a VOR. Which sequence of first-steps is most appropriate in a Part 145 shop?
choices: Replace the receiver immediately and conduct a verification flight-test
Send the aircraft home and ask the pilot to monitor for future occurrences
Swap the NAV head unit with a neighboring aircraft to compare behavior
Inspect antenna, coax, connectors, and power before checking the receiver
practice.explanation: The correct first-step sequence is a systematic physical-layer check: inspect the VOR antenna for physical damage and secure mounting; verify coax continuity and shield integrity; check all connector pins for tightness and corrosion; verify adequate and stable power to the receiver. Only after these items are confirmed good should internal receiver faults be investigated. This is consistent with best Part 145 troubleshooting practice — start with cheap, low-risk checks that resolve most intermittent symptoms before moving to expensive replacement. Ad-hoc swaps, sending the aircraft home without action, and immediate receiver replacement are all poor practice.

preassessment.question: What is a good first step for intermittent VOR NAV flag indications?
choices: Replace the receiver
Return the aircraft to service untouched
Rewrite the logbook
Verify antenna, coax, connectors, and power

questions.learningObjective: Classify an installation as major or minor when a TSO article is installed under an STC that introduces an Aircraft Flight Manual Supplement.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that an installation performed under a Supplemental Type Certificate which requires an Aircraft Flight Manual Supplement is, by its very nature, a major alteration because it changes operating limitations, procedures, or performance information presented to the flight crew.

flashcard.definition: An installation performed under a Supplemental Type Certificate that includes an Aircraft Flight Manual Supplement is considered a major alteration, because the AFMS changes the approved operating limitations or procedures of the aircraft.
flashcard.analogy: Think of the AFMS as a new chapter added to the pilot's rulebook; anything that rewrites the rulebook is always a major change, never a minor tweak.

jeopardy.question: This classification applies to an installation approved by an STC whose paperwork includes an Aircraft Flight Manual Supplement.
choices: Minor alteration
Preventive maintenance
Major alteration
Minor repair
jeopardy.explanation: Any STC installation that carries an AFMS alters the approved operating limitations or flight procedures, which by definition makes it a major alteration per Part 43 Appendix A.

multipleChoice.question: An avionics shop installs a TSO-approved unit using a Supplemental Type Certificate that is issued with an Aircraft Flight Manual Supplement. How should this work be classified?
choices: A minor alteration, because the hardware is TSO approved
Preventive maintenance, because the STC already covers airworthiness
A major alteration, because the STC package changes approved operating data
A minor repair, because the installation follows manufacturer data
multipleChoice.explanation: Per Part 43 Appendix A, a change that affects operating limitations, flight characteristics, or the flight manual is a major alteration. An STC issued with an AFMS alters approved flight data, so the installation is major.

practice.question: Scenario: A technician at a Part 145 shop is installing a new autopilot under an STC. The STC paperwork directs the operator to insert an Aircraft Flight Manual Supplement covering the new operating limitations. How should the job be documented and approved?
choices: As preventive maintenance, with a pilot sign-off
As a minor alteration, logged in the airframe record only
As a major alteration requiring FAA Form 337
As a minor repair, since the STC already provides approval
practice.explanation: An STC installation that includes an AFMS changes approved flight data and is therefore a major alteration. Major alterations must be documented on FAA Form 337 in accordance with Part 43 Section 43.9 and Appendix B.

preassessment.question: If an STC installation requires an Aircraft Flight Manual Supplement, the work is classified as which of the following?
choices: Minor alteration
Minor repair
Preventive maintenance
Major alteration

questions.targetCaetStem: If a TSO part does have an STC for installation and includes a flight manual supplement, is that installation major or minor?

questions.learningObjective: Decide whether a repair station may perform work on an article listed on its capability list when current technical data is not on hand.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that Part 145 Section 145.109 requires a certificated repair station to have the current manufacturer maintenance manual or other approved technical data available when performing work, even if the article is on the capability list; without current data the work may not be performed.

flashcard.definition: Being on the capability list shows a repair station is approved for a class of work, but Part 145 Section 145.109 still requires the current revision of the manufacturer maintenance manual or other approved technical data before any task is performed.
flashcard.analogy: Having a driver's license (capability list) doesn't let you drive without a working car; the current manual is the car that actually takes you down the road.

jeopardy.question: This is required by Section 145.109 before a repair station works on an article, even when the article already appears on the capability list.
choices: A copy of the operator's minimum equipment list
The current revision of the manufacturer maintenance manual
A signed customer work order
A fresh 8130-3 from the vendor
jeopardy.explanation: Section 145.109 requires current technical data, meaning the latest revision of the manufacturer maintenance manual or other FAA-approved data, to be available and used. The capability list alone does not authorize work without that data.

multipleChoice.question: A Part 145 repair station has a particular communications radio listed on its approved capability list, but the most recent revision of the component maintenance manual has not yet been obtained. May the station perform maintenance on that radio?
choices: No, because current technical data is required under Section 145.109
Yes, because the radio is on the capability list
Yes, as long as a previous revision of the manual is on file
No, unless the FAA Principal Inspector grants a one-time waiver
multipleChoice.explanation: Section 145.109 requires the repair station to have and use the current revision of the manufacturer maintenance manual or other approved data. Capability list coverage does not waive that requirement, and older revisions do not satisfy it.

practice.question: Scenario: A customer brings a VHF communications radio to your Part 145 shop for repair. Your capability list covers the unit, but the manufacturer issued a new manual revision last month and your shop has not downloaded it. What is the correct action?
choices: Proceed using the older manual revision since the radio is on the capability list
Release the unit after bench testing even without the new manual
Obtain the current manual revision before starting the repair
Have the technician use personal notes from a recent training class
practice.explanation: Part 145 Section 145.109 requires the station to maintain and use the current manufacturer maintenance manual. The correct action is to obtain the latest revision before beginning work; neither an older revision nor personal notes meets the regulatory requirement.

preassessment.question: A repair station lists an avionics unit on its capability list, but the newest manual revision has not arrived. Can it perform the repair?
choices: No, current technical data is required
Yes, but only bench testing
Yes, the capability list is enough
Yes, if the customer signs a waiver

questions.targetCaetStem: A repair station’s capability list shows a certain radio as “within capabilities,” but the latest manual revision is not available. Can you perform maintenance on it?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the primary industry-accepted wiring standard that a Part 145 avionics shop follows for installation workmanship.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that FAA Advisory Circular AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 (and for new aircraft, AC 43.13-2B) is the most widely accepted data for acceptable methods, techniques, and practices for avionics wiring installation when no specific manufacturer data applies.

flashcard.definition: The FAA Advisory Circular chapter that is the primary acceptable methods, techniques, and practices for aircraft electrical wiring installation, used by Part 145 shops when the manufacturer's instructions do not cover the specific installation.
flashcard.analogy: It is the electrician's 'building code' for airplanes. Follow it and your wiring is presumed airworthy; ignore it and you own every problem that results.

jeopardy.question: This FAA document is the primary acceptable-methods reference for avionics wiring installation workmanship in a Part 145 shop.
choices: RTCA DO-178C
MIL-STD-810
TSO-C91 ELT
jeopardy.explanation: AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 provides the FAA-accepted methods for aircraft electrical wiring installation. DO-178C covers software, MIL-STD-810 covers environmental test, and TSO-C91 is an ELT TSO.

multipleChoice.question: Which reference is considered the most important standard to follow when a Part 145 shop routes and supports new avionics wiring and no specific manufacturer installation data applies?
choices: RTCA DO-160 environmental conditions
MIL-STD-704 aircraft power standards
AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 wiring practices
TSO-C126 for 406 MHz ELTs
multipleChoice.explanation: AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 gives the FAA's accepted methods for aircraft electrical wiring installation and is the default workmanship standard when the aircraft or STC installation instructions do not address a specific detail. The other documents address power quality, environmental qualification, and specific equipment categories.

practice.question: Scenario: You are routing and clamping new wiring for a transponder upgrade in a Part 145 shop. The STC installation instructions do not specify support spacing or bend-radius details. Which document should you follow for the workmanship standard?
choices: AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 for wiring practices
MIL-STD-704 for aircraft power limits
RTCA DO-160 for environmental qualification
The operator's Minimum Equipment List
practice.explanation: When an STC or manufacturer document is silent on a wiring workmanship detail, AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 is the FAA-accepted source for installation methods. The other documents address different aspects of airworthiness and do not cover wiring workmanship.

preassessment.question: Which standard is most important for avionics wiring installation in a Part 145 shop?
choices: MIL-STD-704
RTCA DO-160
TSO-C126
preassessment.explanation: AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 provides FAA-accepted methods for aircraft electrical wiring. MIL-STD-704 covers power characteristics, DO-160 covers environmental test, TSO-C126 is an ELT standard.

questions.targetCaetStem: When installing avionics wiring in a Part 145 shop, which standard is most crucial?

questions.learningObjective: Identify which party in a Part 145 repair station is ultimately responsible for ensuring current technical data is available and used.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that Part 145 places ultimate responsibility on the repair station (through its accountable manager) to ensure current manufacturer and regulatory technical data is available and actually used by personnel performing maintenance.

flashcard.definition: Under Part 145, the repair station itself is responsible for making sure current manufacturer maintenance manuals, service bulletins, and regulatory data are available and used. The accountable manager carries this duty on behalf of the certificate holder.
flashcard.analogy: The certificate holder owns the shop's quality, just like a restaurant owner owns food safety even when line cooks do the cooking.

jeopardy.question: Part 145 assigns ultimate accountability for ensuring technicians have and use current technical data to this role.
choices: The individual technician working at the bench
The repair station, through its accountable manager
The aircraft owner who signs the work order document
The FAA Principal Avionics Inspector for the shop's region
jeopardy.explanation: Part 145 Sections 145.109 and 145.211 make the repair station, acting through its accountable manager and quality system, responsible for maintaining and using current technical data. The technician follows that data, the customer is not responsible, and the FAA inspector oversees compliance rather than supplies the data.

multipleChoice.question: In a Part 145 avionics repair station, who bears the ultimate responsibility for ensuring that current manufacturer manuals and other approved technical data are on hand and actually used during maintenance?
choices: The individual bench technician assigned to the workorder
The assigned FAA Principal Avionics Inspector at the FSDO
The paying customer who signs the work order at intake
The repair station, through its accountable manager
multipleChoice.explanation: Under Part 145, Sections 145.109 and 145.211 place responsibility for current technical data on the repair station and its accountable manager. The technician has a duty to follow the data, but overall responsibility rests with the certificate holder.

practice.question: Scenario: An FAA inspector visits your Part 145 shop and finds that one bench has an outdated manual. The inspector asks who is responsible for current technical data being on hand and used. Which answer is correct?
choices: The technician, because they do the work
The repair station, through the accountable manager
The customer, because they requested the repair
The FAA, because it issued the certificate
practice.explanation: Responsibility for current technical data rests with the certificate holder under Part 145. The accountable manager ensures the quality system keeps manuals current. Individual technicians must follow the data but do not own the obligation to supply it.

preassessment.question: Under Part 145, who is ultimately responsible for ensuring current technical data is available during maintenance?
choices: The repair station
The customer
The FAA inspector
The bench technician

questions.targetCaetStem: Under Part 145, who is responsible for ensuring that the current technical data is available and used during avionics maintenance?

questions.learningObjective: Describe the scope of a Part 145 repair station's capability list and how it is limited by ratings and approved data.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a repair station's capability list identifies the specific makes and models (within the shop's ratings) that the station is authorized to maintain, provided it has the required housing, equipment, personnel, and current technical data for each listed article.

flashcard.definition: A document that identifies the specific articles a Part 145 repair station is authorized to maintain within its approved ratings. Each entry is backed by a self-evaluation showing the shop has the housing, tools, personnel, and current data required for that article.
flashcard.analogy: Think of it as the menu at a restaurant: the ratings are the cuisines you can cook (Italian, sushi), and the capability list is every specific dish you are actually equipped to serve.

jeopardy.question: This list, maintained under Part 145, identifies the specific articles a repair station is authorized to maintain within its approved ratings.
choices: The shop's tool calibration log
The repair station's capability list
The operator's Minimum Equipment List
The FAA Approved Aircraft Inspection Program
jeopardy.explanation: Under Section 145.215, a limited-rated repair station uses a capability list to identify specific articles it is authorized to maintain. Each addition is backed by a self-evaluation demonstrating compliance with Part 145. The other documents serve unrelated purposes.

multipleChoice.question: How is the authorized work scope of a Part 145 repair station most accurately captured in its capability list?
choices: It lists specific articles the station is authorized to maintain within its approved ratings
It replaces the need for manufacturer maintenance manuals on those articles
It lists every aircraft type the shop may return to service in any condition
It grants blanket authority to perform major repairs on any airframe
multipleChoice.explanation: Per Section 145.215, the capability list names the specific makes and models a limited-rated station may maintain inside its ratings. It does not replace manuals, grant airframe authority, or exempt the station from other Part 145 requirements.

practice.question: Scenario: A new avionics technician asks you what their Part 145 shop's capability list actually allows. Which response best explains the scope?
choices: It names the articles the shop may maintain within its ratings
It replaces the need for service bulletins and manufacturer manuals
It grants temporary authority to return major airframe repairs to service
It permits any work on any aircraft the customer brings in
practice.explanation: The capability list under Section 145.215 identifies specific articles a limited-rated station may maintain, conditioned on proper ratings, facilities, personnel, and current data being available. It does not replace manuals or extend authority beyond the ratings on the certificate.

preassessment.question: Which best describes a Part 145 repair station's capability list?
choices: Specific articles the shop may maintain within its ratings
A list of all aircraft types the shop can sign off
A substitute for manufacturer manuals
Authority to perform airframe major repairs

questions.targetCaetStem: Which statement best describes the scope of a repair station’s capabilities under Part 145?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the primary purpose of FAA Form 8130-3, the Authorized Release Certificate / Airworthiness Approval Tag.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that FAA Form 8130-3 certifies that a specific article (new or overhauled) conforms to approved design data and is in a condition for safe operation, providing airworthiness approval for return to service or installation.

flashcard.definition: The Authorized Release Certificate used to provide airworthiness approval for an aircraft part or article, certifying that the item conforms to approved design data and is in a condition for safe operation.
flashcard.analogy: It is a birth certificate plus a clean bill of health for a part: it proves the part is both genuine and airworthy at the moment it leaves the issuer.

jeopardy.question: This is the main reason the FAA issues Form 8130-3 with a new or overhauled component.
choices: To document the component's warranty terms and conditions
To transfer the legal ownership of the article between parties
To list every prior operator or owner of the article over time
To certify airworthiness approval for installation or service
jeopardy.explanation: Form 8130-3 is the Authorized Release Certificate and Airworthiness Approval Tag. Its primary purpose is to certify that the article conforms to approved design data and is in a safe condition. It is not a bill of sale, ownership record, or warranty.

multipleChoice.question: Which statement best captures the main purpose of FAA Form 8130-3, the Authorized Release Certificate?
choices: To transfer legal ownership of an aircraft part between parties
To record pilot training on newly installed cockpit avionics
To provide airworthiness approval for installation or return to service
To replace the maintenance logbook entry for minor repair actions
multipleChoice.explanation: Form 8130-3 provides airworthiness approval by certifying that the listed article conforms to approved design data and is in a condition for safe operation. It does not convey ownership, pilot training, or replace logbook entries.

practice.question: Scenario: A vendor ships your Part 145 shop an overhauled transponder accompanied by an FAA Form 8130-3. When you review the tag, what does that 8130-3 primarily certify about the unit?
choices: That the unit has been legally sold to your shop
That the unit conforms to approved data and is safe to operate
That the unit meets a specific warranty duration
That the unit has been flight tested in an aircraft
